cultural_difference_examples.authority_example:
I grew up in Canada and currently work as a program coordinator for a small human rights NGO that partners with organizations in East Africa and South Asia. I personally lean toward low power distance: I call my executive director by her first name, I’m comfortable questioning decisions in meetings, and I tend to see hierarchy as more about role clarity than about status.

A few years ago, I was coordinating a joint advocacy campaign with a partner organization in Kampala. Our main contact there was a mid-level program manager named Joseph. From my perspective, we were peers, and I expected that we would make most tactical decisions together and then simply inform his director and my executive director as needed.

One incident that stands out: we were drafting a joint statement to be sent to several government ministries. I suggested that we quickly edit the draft together over Zoom and then send it out by the end of the day to meet a media window. Joseph responded that he would first need to share the draft with his director and then possibly with the board chair before anything could be finalized or sent. He also asked if I could get explicit written approval from my executive director for each revision, even minor wording changes.

At the time, I felt frustrated and a bit confused. In my own work culture, that level of sign-off would be considered micromanagement and would slow everything down. I initially reacted by pushing back quite directly, saying something like, “I don’t think we need all of those approvals for this; can we just agree between ourselves and move forward?” I could sense over email that he became uncomfortable and more formal in his tone afterward.

Later, in a call, he explained that in his organization, bypassing formal authority—even when it seems efficient—would be seen as disrespectful and could create serious internal issues for him. Decisions that might be made at my level in my NGO were expected to go two or three levels up in his. I realized I had been imposing my own low power distance expectations on him and unintentionally putting him in a risky position.

If the same situation happened now, I would respond differently. Instead of assuming our roles were equivalent, I would start by asking, “How are decisions like this usually approved in your organization? What steps do you need to follow internally?” I would also explain my own organization’s level of flexibility so we could find a middle ground, like agreeing that he would handle internal approvals on his side while I would do the same on mine, and we’d build more time into the timeline to respect that process. I would frame my suggestions as options rather than challenges to his hierarchy, and I would avoid pressing him to bypass his director. In hindsight, understanding that his cultural norm gave much more weight to senior authority helped me interpret his caution as professionalism rather than resistance or lack of initiative.
cultural_difference_examples.time_example:
I personally have a fairly monochronic approach to time: I like clear agendas, fixed deadlines, and starting meetings on time. I block my calendar, and I get anxious when things slip too much. However, much of my advocacy work is with partners who operate more polychronicly, where time is more flexible and multiple things happen at once.

One concrete example: I was helping coordinate a regional youth consultation in Nairobi related to a UN review process. We had participants and partner staff from Kenya, Tanzania, and Uganda. I had put together a very detailed schedule with time slots down to 15-minute increments (e.g., 10:00–10:15 introductions, 10:15–10:45 small group discussions, etc.). I sent this out in advance and emphasized the importance of punctuality because we had to deliver a summary report within 24 hours.

On the first day, the event was supposed to start at 9:00 AM. By 9:00, only about half the participants and one of the partner coordinators were in the room. The others were still arriving, greeting each other in the hallway, catching up on family news, and having tea. Our local co-host, Sarah, who was Kenyan, didn’t seem worried at all. She suggested we “give people a bit more time to arrive properly” and used the time for informal networking.

Internally, I was stressed. I kept glancing at the clock and mentally recalculating how we would get through the agenda. I whispered to Sarah that we were already 30 minutes behind and that we needed to move people into the room. She calmly replied, “Don’t worry, the important people are here, and the others will join. The conversations they’re having out there are also part of the work.” At the time, that answer made me even more anxious. I interpreted it as a lack of respect for the schedule and for the effort I had put into the plan.

As the day went on, I noticed that group discussions ran long, but they also went deeper than I had anticipated. People were willing to share personal stories and experiences of discrimination that they said they wouldn’t have offered if they had felt rushed. The breaks were also used for networking, side conversations, and informal mentoring between older and younger participants. By the end of the day, we had covered all the main topics, even though almost all the specific time slots had been ignored or rearranged.

Now, looking back, I understand that many of my colleagues were operating in a more polychronic framework where relationships and the quality of interaction are prioritized over sticking rigidly to a clock. If a similar event were happening today, I would still create a structure (because I need it to function), but I would build in more flexibility and explicitly label some times as “approximate.” I would also try to let go of my internal irritation and see the value in people arriving in a more organic way. Instead of whispering anxiously about the time, I might ask, “Which parts of the agenda do you feel are non‑negotiable to keep as they are, and where can we be more flexible?” That way we would balance my monochronic tendencies with their more polychronic approach.
cultural_difference_examples.communication_example:
I grew up in a relatively low-context communication environment, where people tend to say what they mean fairly directly and where written agreements and explicit instructions are valued. In my advocacy work, I’ve often collaborated with colleagues from higher-context cultures, where indirectness, reading between the lines, and non-verbal cues play a much larger role.

A notable example occurred during a collaboration with a South Asian partner organization on a campaign about gender-based violence. We were drafting joint messaging for social media and a briefing note for policymakers. I tend to communicate in a straightforward way, using clear, direct language about problems and demands. Our partner representative, Ananya, often used more nuanced language, with more qualifiers and polite phrases.

During one Zoom call, we were reviewing a draft sentence that I had written: “Local authorities have consistently failed to protect survivors and must be held accountable for ongoing negligence.” Ananya suggested changing it to something like: “Local authorities have faced challenges in consistently protecting survivors, and we encourage them to take stronger and more proactive measures.” To me, this sounded vague and almost like we were letting the authorities off the hook.

I responded by saying, probably too bluntly, “I think this is too soft. If we don’t call out the failure clearly, we’re not really doing advocacy.” I noticed she went quiet and her tone became more formal for the rest of the call. Later, in a private message, she explained that in her context, using very direct, accusatory language toward authorities in a public document could not only backfire strategically but could also endanger their organization’s relationships and even staff safety. She said that stakeholders in her country would understand the criticism from the more nuanced wording; the “reading between the lines” was part of how political communication worked there.

At the time, my initial reaction was to assume that the softer language was simply less effective. I didn’t fully appreciate that her higher-context communication style carried its own internal logic: subtle phrasing could still be understood as strong criticism by people familiar with the local political landscape, while remaining safer and more culturally acceptable.

If I were in the same situation now, I would approach the discussion differently. I would start by asking, “How will policymakers and media in your context interpret this wording? What signals does it send locally?” I would also explain how the language might be perceived by our international supporters and try to find a compromise, perhaps by keeping the more indirect phrasing in local-language materials while using slightly more direct language in a separate, international-facing brief that didn’t put their staff at risk. I would be more attentive to non-verbal cues—like her going quiet—and treat them as important feedback rather than just a personality quirk. In short, I would interpret her indirectness not as avoidance, but as a culturally informed strategy.
cultural_difference_examples.achievement_example:
On the competitive-to-cooperative scale, I personally lean toward cooperative. I care about outcomes and impact, but I’m more motivated by collective success and shared credit than by personal recognition. However, I was raised and educated in a context (Canadian public schools and later a fairly competitive university program) where individual achievement, grades, and leadership titles were heavily emphasized.

A few years ago, I joined a cross-organizational working group on youth civic engagement. The group included staff from NGOs in Europe, Latin America, and Africa. Our goal was to produce a joint toolkit and then present it at an international conference. From the beginning, I suggested we share tasks and rotate facilitation so that everyone would feel ownership. Most people agreed, but one colleague from a large European organization, Mark, approached the project in a more overtly competitive way.

For example, when we were dividing sections of the toolkit, I proposed that we co-author most chapters and list all contributing organizations alphabetically. Mark pushed for his organization to lead the “flagship” sections on strategy and impact measurement, arguing that they had the strongest track record and that it would be important for their branding. He also volunteered himself as the main presenter at the conference, mentioning that he was used to “high-stakes rooms” and implying that others might not handle the pressure as well.

I remember feeling a mix of irritation and self-doubt. Part of me admired his confidence and thought maybe I was being naive about how the sector actually works—that perhaps visibility and competition are necessary to secure funding and influence. Another part of me felt that his approach undermined the spirit of collaboration and disrespected smaller organizations in the group.

Initially, I didn’t handle it very assertively. I tried to smooth things over by suggesting compromises like, “Maybe you can present the main framework, and others can present case studies,” but I didn’t directly address the underlying difference in how we viewed achievement. As a result, he ended up with a disproportionate amount of visibility, and some colleagues privately expressed frustration to me afterward, saying they felt their contributions had been sidelined.

Reflecting later, I realized that Mark was operating from a more competitive cultural and organizational norm where advocacy success is measured in terms of leadership, visibility, and being the “go-to” expert. My more cooperative instinct made me avoid a direct conversation about fairness and power, which in turn contributed to an outcome I wasn’t happy with.

If I were to encounter a similar dynamic now, I would handle it differently. Early in the process, I would propose explicit principles for collaboration, such as: shared authorship, rotation of speaking roles, and transparent criteria for who leads which pieces of work. I would also be more willing to name the tension, perhaps by saying, “I hear that visibility is very important for your organization, and at the same time, several of us want to ensure that this project genuinely reflects a partnership rather than a single lead. Can we brainstorm ways to balance those needs?” By surfacing the difference in assumptions about achievement, we could negotiate a structure that respects both competitive and cooperative motivations. I’ve learned that being cooperative doesn’t have to mean being passive; it can also mean actively shaping spaces so that collective contribution is recognized and rewarded.
